name: Old Ford Factory
alias: Former Ford Motor Factory · the room where Singapore was surrendered
body: On Upper Bukit Timah Road the long art deco facade sits low and pale against the hill, its name still spelled out in raised letters above the entrance. Inside, the air is cool and deliberate, the cool of a museum that knows exactly what it holds. The sense that announces this place is gravity rather than fear: a quiet that settles as you cross the threshold, the weight of a single afternoon that bent the course of a whole island pressing through the polished floors and the careful displays.

This was the first Ford assembly plant in Southeast Asia, and on the fifteenth of February 1942 it became the room in which Lieutenant General Arthur Percival formally surrendered Singapore to General Yamashita Tomoyuki of the Imperial Japanese Army. The fall of Singapore followed, and with it the Japanese Occupation, three and a half years of fear, scarcity, and the killings that mark the city's memory. The factory served as a production and command site during the war. Today it is a national heritage gallery telling that history, and yet visitors and staff have long traded quieter reports: footsteps in empty galleries, the sense of someone standing just behind, a heaviness in the surrender room that some say is simply the room and others say is something that has not left it.

What lingers here is not a single ghost but the residue of a turning point, the knowledge that an ordinary industrial hall absorbed a moment of national catastrophe and never quite gave it back. The displays are measured and factual, and that restraint is part of the unease; the horror is kept behind glass, dated and captioned, which somehow makes it nearer. A concrete detail anchors it: the boardroom-style surrender table and chairs are recreated and displayed for visitors, so that the most famous defeat in Singapore's modern history has a fixed set of furniture you can stand beside and study in the daylight.
note: Documented history is firm and important: the former Ford Motor Factory was the site of the British surrender to Japanese forces on 15 February 1942, and it now operates as a World War II heritage gallery and gazetted national monument. Names, dates, and the role of the building are matters of record and should be stated plainly and respectfully.

The hauntings belong to visitor anecdote and ghost-tour atmosphere, not to the museum's documented account; the footsteps and the heaviness in the surrender room are reported impressions, not verified events. Because this is a place of real suffering during the Occupation, keep the tone sober. The unsettling power of the site comes from genuine history, and that should never be obscured or embellished for effect.
